source: All-In Podcast — Markets: pricing in tariffs, Trump vs Powell, the $10 trillion AGI prize
summary: The US fiscal deficit now materially matters for the first time in decades due to structurally higher interest rates
summary_extended: Baker argues that the US fiscal deficit has become a materially important macro variable for the first time in his investing lifetime, a structural shift driven by the end of the multi-decade declining interest rate regime. For decades, falling rates meant that even as deficits grew, debt service costs remained manageable or even declined in real terms — but with rates now elevated and unlikely to return to prior lows, interest expense is on a trajectory to become the single largest line item in the federal budget, surpassing Medicare, Social Security, and defense spending. Baker frames this as a crowding-out risk: rising debt service consumes an ever-larger share of federal outlays, constraining fiscal flexibility and potentially forcing difficult choices on entitlement and defense spending. He does identify a potential virtuous cycle — if the deficit is meaningfully closed, lower borrowing needs could push rates down, which in turn reduces debt service costs — but warns this window is not indefinite and the math becomes increasingly punishing the longer action is delayed. The thesis carries no specific equity implications; it is a macro/rates structural view with implications for Treasury markets and long-duration assets broadly rather than any identifiable individual company.
detail: Baker argues that the deficit was benign for decades because rates kept falling, but now that rates are elevated and unlikely to return to prior lows, interest expense is on a path to become the largest line item in the federal budget, crowding out Medicare, Social Security, and defense. He sees a virtuous cycle available if the deficit is closed — lower rates feeding lower debt service — but warns the window is not indefinite.
confidence_evidence: Baker speaks at length with specific numerical framing and historical context; uses 'it really matters' and 'finally does matter after really never mattering in my political lifetime'
quote_fragment: the deficit finally matters after really never mattering in my political lifetime
